accurate.why: All claims match the bill text and accurate context (FDA administers FD&C Act approvals; Section 546 trains first responders to administer overdose-reversal drugs).

no_hallucinations.why: References to FDA and the overdose-reversal training program are accurate real-world context, not fabrications, and nothing contradicts the bill.

states_purpose.why: Clearly states the bill reauthorizes and expands the Section 546 first responder training grant program.

changes_to_existing_law.why: Correctly identifies amendments to Section 546 of the Public Health Service Act (42 U.S.C. 290ee-1).

exceptions_conditions.why: The bill contains no material exceptions, eligibility carve-outs, or conditions beyond the amendments described.

effective_dates.why: Correctly notes the funding period of fiscal years 2026 through 2030.

major_provisions_covered.why: Captures the expanded substance scope, removal of opioid-only focus, and increased reauthorization amount.

objective_tone.why: The summary is neutral and descriptive with no editorializing.

coherent.why: The summary is well-organized and internally consistent.

concise.why: The summary is appropriately compact without padding or repetition.

correct_entities.why: Correctly names the Public Health Service Act, Section 546, FDA, and Tribes/Tribal capitalization edit.

correct_figures.why: Figures ($57,000,000 for FY2026-2030 replacing $36,000,000 for FY2019-2023) match the bill text exactly.
